[{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. var_functions.query_db:10.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load books data from file
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:8']
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data from file
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(books_data)} books and {len(reviews_data)} reviews")

# Extract publication year from books details
books_with_years = []
year_pattern = r'(\d{4})'

for book in books_data:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    
    # Find year in details string
    match = re.search(year_pattern, details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year
        })

print(f"Found publication years for {len(books_with_years)} books")
print(f"Year range: {min([b['year'] for b in books_with_years])} - {max([b['year'] for b in books_with_years])}")

# Group reviews by purchase_id (book_id)
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    reviews_by_book[purchase_id].append(rating)

print(f"Found reviews for {len(reviews_by_book)} distinct books")

# Join books with their reviews and calculate decade stats
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'total_reviews': 0, 'books': set()})

for book in books_with_years:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    year = book['year']
    decade = (year // 10) * 10  # e.g., 2014 -> 2010
    
    if book_id in reviews_by_book:
        ratings = reviews_by_book[book_id]
        decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += sum(ratings)
        decade_stats[decade]['total_reviews'] += len(ratings)
        decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

# Calculate average rating per decade and filter by at least 10 distinct books
decade_averages = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['total_reviews']
        decade_averages.append({
            'decade': f"{decade}s",
            'decade_start': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': stats['total_reviews']
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_averages.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"Found {len(decade_averages)} decades with at least 10 books")
for d in decade_averages[:5]:
    print(f"{d['decade']}: avg rating {d['avg_rating']}, {d['num_books']} books, {d['total_reviews']} reviews")

# Get the decade with highest average rating
if decade_averages:
    best_decade = decade_averages[0]
    result = best_decade['decade']
else:
    result = "No decade found with at least 10 books"

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load books data from file
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:8']
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data from file
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(books_data)} books and {len(reviews_data)} reviews")

# Extract publication year from books details
books_with_years = []
year_pattern = r'(\d{4})'

for book in books_data:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    
    # Find year in details string
    match = re.search(year_pattern, details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year
        })

print(f"Found publication years for {len(books_with_years)} books")
print(f"Year range: {min([b['year'] for b in books_with_years])} - {max([b['year'] for b in books_with_years])}")

# Group reviews by purchase_id (book_id)
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    reviews_by_book[purchase_id].append(rating)

print(f"Found reviews for {len(reviews_by_book)} distinct books")

# Join books with their reviews and calculate decade stats
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'total_reviews': 0, 'books': set()})

for book in books_with_years:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    year = book['year']
    decade = (year // 10) * 10  # e.g., 2014 -> 2010
    
    if book_id in reviews_by_book:
        ratings = reviews_by_book[book_id]
        decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += sum(ratings)
        decade_stats[decade]['total_reviews'] += len(ratings)
        decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

# Calculate average rating per decade and filter by at least 10 distinct books
decade_averages = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['total_reviews']
        decade_averages.append({
            'decade': f"{decade}s",
            'decade_start': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': stats['total_reviews']
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_averages.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"Found {len(decade_averages)} decades with at least 10 books")
for d in decade_averages[:5]:
    print(f"{d['decade']}: avg rating {d['avg_rating']}, {d['num_books']} books, {d['total_reviews']} reviews")

# Get the decade with highest average rating
if decade_averages:
    best_decade = decade_averages[0]
    result = best_decade['decade']
else:
    result = "No decade found with at least 10 books"

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:10.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data from storage
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:8']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Books: {len(books_data)}, Reviews: {len(reviews_data)}")

# Extract publication years with more flexible pattern matching
books_with_years = []
year_patterns = [
    r'published[^\d]{0,100}?(\d{4})',  # published on/in... year
    r'released[^\d]{0,100}?(\d{4})',   # released on... year
    r'edition[^\d]{0,100}?(\d{4})',    # edition... year
]

for book in books_data:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '').lower()
    
    # Try to find a 4-digit year that looks like a publication year
    year = None
    for pattern in year_patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1800 <= year <= 2023:  # Reasonable range
                break
    
    # If pattern matching failed, look for any 4-digit year in 1800-2023 range
    if year is None:
        years = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
        for y in years:
            y_int = int(y)
            if 1800 <= y_int <= 2023 and y_int != 8082:  # Exclude weird outlier
                year = y_int
                break
    
    if year is not None:
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'index': int(book_id.split('_')[1])  # Extract numeric index
        })

print(f"Books with extracted years: {len(books_with_years)}")

# Now try to find mapping between book_id and purchase_id
# Let's look at the numeric indices
review_indices = defaultdict(int)
for review in reviews_data[:100000]:  # Sample subset to find pattern
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id', '')
    if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
        try:
            idx = int(purchase_id.split('_')[1])
            review_indices[idx] += 1
        except:
            pass

# Show distribution
indices = sorted(review_indices.items(), key=lambda x: x[0])[:20]
print(f"Sample purchase_id indices and frequencies: {indices}")

# Check if book indices align with purchase indices
book_indices = [b['index'] for b in books_with_years]
print(f"Book indices range: {min(book_indices) if book_indices else 'None'} - {max(book_indices) if book_indices else 'None'}")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps("completed analysis"))

import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:32']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Books loaded: {len(books_data)}")
print(f"Reviews loaded: {len(reviews_data)}")
print(f"Sample book: {books_data[0]}")
print(f"Sample review: {reviews_data[0]}")

# Try simple approach: check if book_id == purchase_id
book_ids = set(b['book_id'] for b in books_data)
purchase_ids = set(r['purchase_id'] for r in reviews_data)
direct_matches = book_ids.intersection(purchase_ids)
print(f"Direct matches: {len(direct_matches)}")

# Try numeric matching approach
book_nums = {}
for book in books_data:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    if book_id.startswith('bookid_'):
        try:
            num = int(book_id.split('_')[1])
            book_nums[num] = book
        except:
            pass

review_nums = {}
for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
        try:
            num = int(purchase_id.split('_')[1])
            review_nums[num] = review
        except:
            pass

print(f"Books with numeric IDs: {len(book_nums)}")
print(f"Reviews with numeric IDs: {len(review_nums)}")

# Check overlap of numeric indices
common_nums = set(book_nums.keys()).intersection(set(review_nums.keys()))
print(f"Common numeric indices: {len(common_nums)}")
print(f"Sample common indices: {sorted(list(common_nums))[:10]}")

result = f"Analysis: {len(books_data)} books, {len(reviews_data)} reviews, {len(common_nums)} overlapping IDs"
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load full data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:32']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(books_data)} books and {len(reviews_data)} reviews")

# Extract publication year from book details
books_by_id = {}
year_pattern = r'(\d{4})'

for book in books_data:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    
    # Find year in details - look for 4-digit numbers in reasonable range
    year = None
    years = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    for y in years:
        y_int = int(y)
        if 1800 <= y_int <= 2023:
            year = y_int
            break
    
    if year and book_id.startswith('bookid_'):
        try:
            book_num = int(book_id.split('_')[1])
            books_by_id[book_num] = {
                'book_id': book_id,
                'year': year,
                'numeric_id': book_num
            }
        except:
            pass

print(f"Books with valid years: {len(books_by_id)}")

# Group reviews by numeric ID
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    
    if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
        try:
            purchase_num = int(purchase_id.split('_')[1])
            reviews_by_book[purchase_num].append(rating)
        except:
            pass

print(f"Books with reviews: {len(reviews_by_book)}")

# Calculate decade statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'total_reviews': 0, 'books': set(), 'ratings': []})

for book_num, book_info in books_by_id.items():
    if book_num in reviews_by_book:
        ratings = reviews_by_book[book_num]
        year = book_info['year']
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        
        decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += sum(ratings)
        decade_stats[decade]['total_reviews'] += len(ratings)
        decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_num)
        decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].extend(ratings)

print(f"Decades found: {sorted(decade_stats.keys())}")

# Calculate averages and filter by at least 10 books
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['total_reviews']
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': f"{decade}s",
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': stats['total_reviews']
        })

print(f"Decades with >=10 books: {len(decade_results)}")

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print("\nTop decades by average rating:")
for i, d in enumerate(decade_results[:10]):
    print(f"{i+1}. {d['decade']}: {d['avg_rating']} avg rating ({d['num_books']} books, {d['total_reviews']} reviews)")

# Get the best decade
best_decade = decade_results[0]['decade'] if decade_results else "None found"

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(best_decade))

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load full data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:32']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(books_data)} books and {len(reviews_data)} reviews")

# Extract publication year from book details
books_by_id = {}
for book in books_data:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    
    # Find 4-digit year in reasonable range
    year = None
    years = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    for y in years:
        y_int = int(y)
        if 1800 <= y_int <= 2023:
            year = y_int
            break
    
    if year and book_id.startswith('bookid_'):
        try:
            book_num = int(book_id.split('_')[1])
            books_by_id[book_num] = {'book_id': book_id, 'year': year}
        except:
            pass

print(f"Books with valid years: {len(books_by_id)}")

# Group reviews by numeric ID
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id', '')
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    
    if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
        try:
            purchase_num = int(purchase_id.split('_')[1])
            reviews_by_book[purchase_num].append(rating)
        except:
            pass

print(f"Books with reviews: {len(reviews_by_book)}")

# Calculate decade statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'total_reviews': 0, 'books': set()})

for book_num, book_info in books_by_id.items():
    if book_num in reviews_by_book:
        ratings = reviews_by_book[book_num]
        year = book_info['year']
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        
        decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += sum(ratings)
        decade_stats[decade]['total_reviews'] += len(ratings)
        decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_num)

# Calculate averages and filter by at least 10 books
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['total_reviews']
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': str(decade) + 's',
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': stats['total_reviews']
        })

print(f"Decades with >=10 books: {len(decade_results)}")

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

if decade_results:
    best_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    print(f"Best decade: {best_decade} with rating {decade_results[0]['avg_rating']}")
else:
    best_decade = "No decade found"
    print("No decade found with at least 10 books")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(best_decade))
